If it's of a l- sort of little bit larger size project, it also has to provide storage so that we're not creating a lot of runoff that wasn't coming off that property before it was constructed. And it also means that we're tasked with making sure that that continues to function after it's built and that those stormwater systems are being maintained. The last one I'll talk about is pollution prevention. So this applies to city-owned properties.

**[31:54]** This is the municipal services facility, the transfer station, all of our larger municipal properties. What's involved is knowing what pollution sources are on the site. It also means that every single street is swept at least once a year. That's a Public Works function, and they do a great job at it. My group inspects and cleans every single catch basin in the

**[32:39]** city every year, which exceeds what DEP asks us to do. That's 2,800 catch basins. Again, yes, thank you. Thank you. I don't do it, but the crew does it, and they're fabulous. All the material we take out of those catch basins means that material is not getting into the water bodies. So that's a not glamorous job, okay, but it's very important to do. And we also inspect every one of those when we do it. So we're looking for odors or sheen or discoloration or foaming, which would be an indication that there's a source of pollution coming in. So it's an important task for that reason as well. Something else I'll mention about pollution prevention is the Public Works Department is very involved in decreasing how much salt is being put down for every storm. And that's a lot of different things that go into that.

**[33:27]** There is annual training for all of the Public Works truck drivers, the plow truck operators. But those trucks are also calibrated so that they know exactly how many tons of salt are being applied per lane mile. The settings are locked in so that they can't be changed. The department pre-wets the salt by coating it with a brine solution so that when the salt comes out of the back of the truck, it doesn't bounce and go off the side of the road. It sticks better on the roadway. It's not high tech, but boy, does that make a difference in how much salt you're putting down. We're also looking at new technologies. We've together Water Resources and Public Works applied for a grant for a new type of technology to pre-treat the road before a storm event with liquid brine to prevent ice from forming in the first

**[34:13]** place. So that will go very far to reduce the total amount of salt we put down. So those are just some of the tools that we do and some of the tools that we, some of the things that we look at at our municipal facilities. The last thing I'll talk about, and this is a new element of the MS4 permit, and that is our impaired waters program. So in the city, unfortunately, we have five different streams that are considered urban impaired streams. And that means that they've been impacted by development. It means that there's more impervious area that is in the watershed of those streams. More impervious area means more pollution. It means more salt. And when you have a lot of impervious area in a watershed, it's just

**[35:00]** more stress on that stream. So we are required to look at each one of those, assess what is causing those impairments, and then try to come up with projects to mitigate those impairments. Willard Beach was added to the list of impaired waters as an impaired marine water in 2024. I think you know a lot of, all know that we're doing a lot of work at Willard Beach.
